nativeRange.habitat: Fast-flowing rivers with rocky substrates near the source and muddy/sandy substrates with dense aquatic vegetation in slower reaches; overhanging shore vegetation; clear, well-oxygenated water in the Río Coy system of the upper Río Pánuco basin, San Luis Potosí

decorPreferences: dense planting along sides and background (Java moss, hornwort, water sprite)
smooth rocks and pebbles replicating fast-water biotope
overhanging or floating plants for surface cover
gentle to moderate current from filtration

diet.notes: Wild fish graze on aufwuchs, microalgae, and zooplankton. In the aquarium, offer a varied diet of high-quality micro-pellets or flake supplemented with small live and frozen foods — daphnia, artemia/brine shrimp, cyclops, and mosquito larvae. Occasional spirulina-based or algae flake helps replicate the aufwuchs component. Juveniles benefit from freshly hatched artemia nauplii and finely powdered dry food.

goodWith: other small peaceful livebearers of similar size
small rasboras and tetras that prefer harder water
avoidWith: larger, boisterous fish that outcompete at feeding
large shrimp species likely to predate fry (neocaridina acceptable with dense planting)

summary: A tiny, cool-tolerant livebearer from the Río Pánuco basin of Mexico. Males display a short sword extension and are famous in evolutionary biology for a genetically determined size polymorphism — large "courter" males and small "sneaker" males pursue completely different reproductive tactics, making this species a textbook model for alternative mating strategies and female mate-choice research.
careNotes:
The Pygmy Swordtail (Xiphophorus multilineatus) is a small but scientifically celebrated livebearer endemic to the Río Coy system of the upper Río Pánuco drainage in San Luis Potosí, Mexico — a fast-flowing, spring-fed river system that stays relatively cool and clean. This cooler, hard-water origin means the species tolerates temperatures as low as 18°C and does well at 22–25°C, making it suitable for unheated or lightly heated setups in temperate rooms — a notable advantage over most tropical livebearers. Hard, alkaline water (pH 7.0–8.0, gH 10–25) is essential; soft or acidic conditions are poorly tolerated and quickly increase disease susceptibility.

Despite the modest 60 L minimum, a species-only or near-species-only setup is strongly recommended. X. multilineatus is exceptionally stress-sensitive: it readily hides among plants and may starve if outcompeted by faster or more assertive tankmates. Excellent filtration producing a gentle current, frequent small water changes (20–25% weekly), and stable parameters are more important for this species than for most hobbyist livebearers.

The species is best known for a striking male size polymorphism with a genetic basis on the Y chromosome. "Courter" males (25–42 mm) are larger, deeper-bodied, develop the characteristic high back and bold zigzag midlateral striping, and employ persistent courtship displays to attract females. "Sneaker" males (22–28 mm) are more fusiform and use a mix of courtship and opportunistic sneak-chase copulation attempts. Females consistently prefer courter males, but the strength of that preference varies with female genotype and growth rate — a system that has generated decades of research into sexual selection and female mate-choice. In the aquarium, maintaining multiple females per male reduces harassment; a ratio of 2–3 females per male is advisable. A small group (1–2 males, 3–5 females) thrives in 60–80 L with dense planting.

The common trade name “Pygmy Swordtail” is shared with the related X. pygmaeus (Slender Pygmy Swordtail); X. multilineatus is distinguished by its high back in large males, prominent vertical flank barring, and slightly stockier build. See also: [swordtail] (X. helleri) — the common large swordtail; [platy] (X. maculatus) — a close Poeciliidae relative.
breedingNotes: X. multilineatus is a livebearer with a gestation period of approximately 24–28 days, producing small broods of 5–20 fry (typically 10–15). Females can store sperm and produce several successive broods from a single mating. Fry are relatively large at birth and generally ignored by adults in a well-planted tank, though a separate breeding or fry container improves survival rates. Juveniles reach sexual maturity in approximately 4 months, with full adult coloration in courter males developing within the first year. Because male size class (courter vs. sneaker) is genetically determined and expressed during growth rather than conditionally at maturity, keepers interested in maintaining both morphs should retain fish from multiple broods to observe the polymorphism. Breeding is rated moderate — the species breeds readily under good conditions but its stress sensitivity and preference for a species-only setup means it rarely produces the large numbers seen with hardier livebearers.

primary.name: FishBase — Xiphophorus multilineatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Confirmed max TL males 4.5 cm, females 5.0 cm; temperature 23–25°C; native range Rio Coy of Rio Panuco basin, San Luis Potosi, Mexico; habitat fast-flowing rivers with dense aquatic vegetation; livebearer gestation 24–28 days, 10–15 young; taxonomy Poeciliidae, Cyprinodontiformes; described Rauchenberger, Kallman & Morizot 1990.

additional.name: AquaInfo — Xiphophorus multilineatus
additional.notes: Confirmed minimum tank 60 cm × 40 cm × 30 cm; temperature 23–25°C; diet live/frozen artemia, cyclops, daphnia, mosquito larvae and high-protein dry food; stress sensitivity requiring species-only setup; males 25–40 mm, females 40 mm; broods 10–20 fry monthly; juveniles mature in 4 months, full color in 1 year.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Pygmy Swordtail, Xiphophorus multilineatus
additional.notes: Confirmed temperature 22–26°C (71.5–78°F); pH 7.0–8.0; hard alkaline water requirement; diet aufwuchs, algae, zooplankton in wild; accepts daphnia, brine shrimp, flake in aquarium; rocky boulder-filled tank with good water movement; recommended more females than males; broods 10–20 fry.

additional.name: British Livebearer Association — Xiphophorus multilineatus
additional.notes: Confirmed endemic range: Rio Coy system including Arroyo Tambaque and Arroyo Oxitipa, Rio Panuco drainage, SLP Mexico; four distinct male size variants; habitat fast-flowing river with dense plant growth and overhanging vegetation; fry large and normally left alone by parents; requires large well-planted tank with good filtration and aeration.

additional.name: PLOS ONE — Genotype and growth rate influence female mate preference in Xiphophorus multilineatus
additional.notes: Peer-reviewed source for male polymorphism biology. Confirmed courter males (25–42 mm, deeper-bodied, fixed courtship) vs. sneaker males (22–28 mm, fusiform, use both courtship and sneak-chase copulation); male size class determined by Mc4r gene alleles on Y chromosome; female preference strength influenced by female genotype and growth rate.

fish.breedingNotes: Livebearer; gestation 24–28 days; broods of 5–20 fry (typically 10–15). Females store sperm for multiple successive broods. Fry are large at birth and generally left alone in planted tanks. Juvenile maturity ~4 months; full courter-male coloration within 1 year. Both male morphs (courter and sneaker) should be expected in a breeding group; male size class is genetically fixed, not environmentally induced.
